nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on steep outer reef slopes and outer channel walls at depths of about 0–65 m (most commonly 0–15 m), hovering close to coral or rubble at the base of dropoffs either solitary or in small loosely scattered groups.

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding primarily on small benthic crustaceans. In captivity generally adjusts readily to prepared aquarium foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped krill / squid / clam / mussel, and quality carnivore-formulated marine pellet or frozen preparations. A varied, pigment-rich diet is commonly considered helpful in maintaining the bright magenta coloration, which has been reported to fade on monotonous diets. Generally advised to feed one to two small meals per day.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2134x1601. Tack-sharp full lateral profile of a live, vibrant, uniformly bright magenta-purple individual with blue-ringed eye and no black eye stripe or dark fin edging (diagnostic vs. Orchid Dottyback). Best hero shot.
Brian Gratwicke; 2664x1674. Sharp full lateral view against soft-coral reef, uniform magenta-pink coloration, whole fish in frame. Different individual/setting for variety.
Rickard Zerpe; 2147x1610. Dramatic sharp lateral shot on black background showing brilliant magenta body with blue dorsal and caudal fin highlights; striking alternate angle.

summary: A small, brilliantly magenta-purple dottyback from the western Pacific, generally considered hardy and beginner-accessible but notably semi-aggressive toward similar-shaped tankmates. Best kept singly in a reef tank with abundant rockwork and a tight lid.
careNotes:
The Purple Dottyback — widely sold under the alternate names Magenta Dottyback and Strawberry Dottyback — is one of the more commonly kept small dottybacks in the marine trade. Although the species was reclassified from Pseudochromis porphyreus to Pictichromis porphyrea, the older genus name remains in widespread use by retailers and both names refer to the same fish. It is generally considered hardy, accepts captive foods readily, and tolerates the standard reef parameter window.

Despite its small adult size, the Purple Dottyback is reliably reported to be semi-aggressive and territorial. While it tends to be somewhat less belligerent than some of its relatives in the family, it is still widely regarded as prone to harassing tankmates that are similar in shape, size, or color — particularly peaceful species introduced after the dottyback has established a territory. Conspecific aggression is high: only one should be kept per tank, and it should not be combined with other dottybacks or similarly shaped fish. It is generally advised to add the Purple Dottyback last when stocking a community reef.

Reef compatibility with corals and most sessile invertebrates is generally considered good, but the species has been reported to prey on small ornamental shrimp. Larger cleaner shrimp and fire shrimp typically coexist safely.

Plenty of live rock with caves, crevices, and overhangs is important to provide a defendable home territory. The species is also a documented jumper, so a tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended.

Lifespan in the aquarium is reported to fall in the range of 5–8 years, consistent with the general longevity of the family.

Keepers sometimes confuse this species with the Orchid Dottyback (Pictichromis fridmani). The Purple/Magenta Dottyback lacks the black eye stripe and dark-purple fin edging of the Orchid, appearing instead as a more uniformly bright magenta.
breedingNotes: Spawning has been reported in home aquariums, though larval rearing is generally considered extremely challenging due to the small larval size. Like other dottybacks, the male is generally expected to prepare a cave or crevice and court a female, who deposits an egg ball inside the hiding site that the male typically guards. Mature females may appear visibly fuller-bodied than males. Commercially captive-bred specimens are produced in limited numbers and occasionally appear in the trade, though wild-caught fish appear to represent the majority of those sold. Home-aquarium pair formation is impractical in normal community-sized tanks because conspecifics cannot be safely combined in modest volumes.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed common/scientific name (Pseudochromis porphyreus — legacy genus still used by retailer; current valid name is Pictichromis porphyrea per FishBase), family Pseudochromidae, care level Easy, temperament Semi-Aggressive, reef-safe Yes, diet carnivore, max size 3 in, minimum tank 30 US gal, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), and the warning about aggression toward other Pseudochromis or similarly shaped fish. Reached via the /collections/dottybacks index per the manifest.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Secondary source — confirmed Pseudochromis porphyreus / Pictichromis porphyrea, Indo-Pacific origin, max size 2.5 in, minimum tank 24 US gal, reef-compatible, beginner care level, semi-aggressive temperament, carnivorous diet (krill, shrimp, squid, clam, mussel), strong caution that the species 'can often be quite the bullies' and is unsuitable for peaceful community tanks with timid feeders or smaller fish. Confirmed distinguishing trait vs. Orchid Dottyback (lacks black eye stripe and dark purple fin edging).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc), valid genus reassignment from Pseudochromis to Pictichromis, authority (Lubbock & Goldman, 1974), accepted scientific common name 'Magenta dottyback', max length 6.0 cm TL, depth range 0–65 m (commonly 0–15 m), wild temperature 25.5–29.4°C (mean 28.7°C), native distribution (Philippines to Samoa, Ryukyu Islands, Moluccas, Admiralty Islands), trophic level 3.5 (carnivore), IUCN Least Concern (2015), and habitat description (steep outer reef slopes and channel walls, solitary or small loose groups).

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk (Maidenhead Aquatics)
additional.notes: Third confirming source for max size 6 cm, water parameters (22–28°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), temperament ('fairly feisty', unsuitable with peaceful species), male territoriality, the documented jumping behavior that justifies the escapeRisk: high rating and tight-fitting cover recommendation, the small-shrimp predation caveat for reef compatibility, and a note that home-aquarium spawning has occurred (larval rearing extremely challenging).

additional.name: Reef Builders (The Magnificent Magenta Dottyback)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size ~2.5 in TL, 20–30 US gal minimum, semi-aggressive temperament that is 'still all dottyback' but milder than congeners, high conspecific aggression toward similar-shaped fish, documented jumping incident (escape through cover gap), wild diet of small benthic crustaceans, and a caution that the species attacks more peaceful fish introduced after it becomes established.

fish.breedingNotes: The Purple Dottyback is a cave-spawning species in which the male is generally expected to prepare a hiding site and court a female, who deposits an egg ball inside the cave that the male typically guards. Home-aquarium spawning has been reported, but larval rearing is generally considered extremely challenging due to the small larval size. Commercial captive-bred specimens exist but are less common in the trade than for some other members of the family Pseudochromidae. Pair formation in normal community-sized tanks is impractical because conspecifics cannot be safely combined.
